questions.question: State and prove the Division Algorithm for integers.
keyPoints: State: For integers a, b (b > 0), unique q, r exist with a = bq + r and 0 ≤ r < b
Proof uses Well-Ordering Principle or induction
Show uniqueness: assume two representations and prove q and r must be equal

questions.question: Find GCD(420, 126) using the Euclidean Algorithm. Show all steps.
keyPoints: 420 ÷ 126 = 3 remainder 42
126 ÷ 42 = 3 remainder 0
GCD = 42
Verify: 42 divides both 420 and 126, and any common divisor also divides 42

questions.question: Define prime numbers and prove that there are infinitely many primes.
keyPoints: Prime: natural number > 1 with no divisors except 1 and itself
Proof: Assume finite primes p1, p2, ..., pn
Consider N = p1p2...pn + 1
N is not divisible by any prime (leaves remainder 1)
Contradiction: N must have a prime factor, so primes are infinite

questions.question: State and prove Bezout's Identity. Explain its significance.
keyPoints: Statement: For any a, b ∈ Z, ∃x, y ∈ Z such that ax + by = gcd(a,b)
Proved using Extended Euclidean Algorithm
Shows GCD can be expressed as linear combination
Forms basis for finding modular inverses

questions.question: If gcd(a, b) = 1, prove that gcd(a + b, ab) = 1.
keyPoints: Let d = gcd(a+b, ab)
Any prime dividing d must divide a+b and ab
If p|a and p|ab, then p cannot divide a+b (would divide b)
Contradiction unless p=1
Therefore d = 1

questions.question: State and prove the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic.
keyPoints: Every n > 1 can be written as product of primes (existence)
Prove by strong induction: base case n=2 is prime
Assume true for all < n, prove for n
Uniqueness: if n = p1p2...pk = q1q2...qm, show k=m and primes match up

questions.question: Find the LCM and GCD of 72 and 120. Verify that GCD × LCM = product of the numbers.
keyPoints: Prime factorization: 72 = 2³×3², 120 = 2³×3×5

questions.question: Explain the Euclidean Algorithm in detail. Use it to find GCD(312, 156). Also explain the Extended Euclidean Algorithm with an example.
keyPoints: Euclidean Algorithm: Replace (a,b) with (b, a mod b) repeatedly until remainder is 0
Extended: Also find x,y such that ax+by=gcd(a,b)
Used in cryptography for modular inverses

questions.question: (a) Define co-prime numbers. (b) Prove that if a and b are co-prime and a divides bc, then a divides c (Euclid's Lemma). (c) Using this, prove that √2 is irrational.
keyPoints: (a) gcd(a,b)=1
(b) Since gcd(a,b)=1, by Bezout: ax+by=1. Multiply by c: acx+bcy=c. a divides both terms, so a|c
(c) Assume √2 = p/q in lowest terms. Then 2q² = p², so p is even, p=2k. Then 2q²=4k², q²=2k², q is even. Contradiction: p,q have common factor 2. Therefore √2 is irrational.

questions.question: (a) State and prove that gcd(a,b) × lcm(a,b) = |a × b|. (b) Find all integer solutions to 12x + 18y = 6.
keyPoints: (a) Let gcd(a,b)=d, write a=dm, b=dn with gcd(m,n)=1. Then lcm(a,b)=dmn. So product = d²mn = (dm)(dn) = ab
(b) First find gcd(12,18)=6. Divide by 6: 2x + 3y = 1. Particular solution: x₀=-1, y₀=1. General solution: x = -1 + 3t, y = 1 - 2t for any integer t.
